front: What is the formal definition of a group?
back: A group (G, ∗) is a set G, closed under a binary operation ∗, such that the following axioms are satisfied: 1) Associativity: (a ∗ b) ∗ c = a ∗ (b ∗ c) for all a, b, c in G. 2) Identity: There exists an element e in G such that e ∗ a = a ∗ e = a for all a in G. 3) Inverse: For each a in G, there exists an element a⁻¹ in G such that a ∗ a⁻¹ = a⁻¹ ∗ a = e.

front: What is an abelian (or commutative) group?
back: A group (G, ∗) is called an abelian group if, in addition to the group axioms, the operation ∗ is commutative. That is, a ∗ b = b ∗ a for all a, b in G.

front: What is the definition of the order of a group and the order of an element?
back: The order of a group (G, ∗) is the number of elements in G, denoted by |G|. The order of an element a in G is the smallest positive integer n such that aⁿ = e (the identity). If no such integer exists, the element has infinite order.

front: What is a subgroup? Provide the necessary and sufficient conditions for a subset H of a group G to be a subgroup.
back: A subset H of a group (G, ∗) is a subgroup of G if H is itself a group under the operation ∗ inherited from G. The conditions are: 1) Closure: For all a, b in H, a ∗ b is in H. 2) Identity: The identity element e of G is in H. 3) Inverses: For each a in H, the inverse a⁻¹ is in H. This is often summarized as the one-step subgroup test: a, b in H implies a ∗ b⁻¹ is in H.
A group G is cyclic if there exists an element a in G such that G = {aⁿ | n ∈ ℤ}. The element a is called a generator of G, and we write G = ⟨a⟩. Every cyclic group is abelian.
